From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Community of license](/w/index.php?title=Community_of_license&redirect=no "Community of license"))

Community that a radio or television station is licensed to serve

In North American (U.S., Canadian, and Mexican) broadcasting, a **city of license** or **community of license** is the community that a [radio station](/wiki/Radio_station "Radio station") or [television station](/wiki/Television_station "Television station") is officially licensed to serve by that country's broadcast regulator.

In North American broadcast law, the concept of *community of license* dates to the early days of [AM radio](/wiki/AM_radio "AM radio") broadcasting. The requirement that a broadcasting station operate a *main studio* within a prescribed distance of the community which the station is licensed to serve appears in [U.S. law](/wiki/United_States_federal_law "United States federal law") as early as 1939.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Various specific obligations have been applied to broadcasters by governments to fulfill [public policy](/wiki/Public_policy "Public policy") objectives of broadcast [localism](/wiki/Localism_(politics) "Localism (politics)"), both in radio and later also in television, based on the legislative presumption that a broadcaster fills a similar role to that held by community newspaper publishers.

## United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_of_license&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: United States")]

In the United States, the [Communications Act of 1934](/wiki/Communications_Act_of_1934 "Communications Act of 1934") requires that "the Commission shall make such distribution of licenses, frequencies, hours of operation, and of power among the several States and communities as to provide a fair, efficient, and equitable distribution of radio service to each of the same."[[2]](#cite_note-2) The [Federal Communications Commission](/wiki/Federal_Communications_Commission "Federal Communications Commission") interprets this as requiring that every broadcast station "be licensed to the principal community or other political subdivision which it primarily serves."[[3]](#cite_note-3) For each broadcast service, the FCC defines a standard for what it means to serve a community; for example, commercial [FM radio](/wiki/FM_radio "FM radio") stations are required to provide a [field strength](/wiki/Field_strength "Field strength") of at least 3.16 [millivolts](/wiki/Millivolt "Millivolt") per meter (mV/m) over the entire land area of the community,[[4]](#cite_note-4) whereas [non-commercial educational](/wiki/Non-commercial_educational "Non-commercial educational") FM stations need only provide a field strength of 1 mV/m over 50% of the community's population.[[5]](#cite_note-5) This electric field contour is called the "principal community contour".

The [Federal Communications Commission](/wiki/Federal_Communications_Commission "Federal Communications Commission") (FCC) makes other requirements on stations relative to their communities of license; these requirements have varied over time. One example is the requirement for stations to identify themselves, by [call sign](/wiki/North_American_call_sign "North American call sign") and community, at sign-on, sign-off, and at the top of every hour of operation.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Other current requirements include providing a local telephone number in the community's calling area (or else a toll-free number).[[7]](#cite_note-7) The former requirement to (in most cases) maintain an official main studio within 25 miles of the community's geographic center was discontinued in October 2017 when the regulation was amended.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Policy and regulatory issues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_of_license&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Policy and regulatory issues")]

### Nominal main studio requirements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_of_license&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Nominal main studio requirements")]

The requirement that a station maintain a main studio within a station's primary coverage area or within a maximum distance of the community of license originated in an era in which stations were legally required to generate local content and the majority of a station's local, non-network programming was expected to originate in one central studio location. In this context, the view of broadcast regulators held that an expedient way to ensure that content broadcast reflected the needs of a local community was to allocate local broadcast stations and studios to each individual city.

The nominal main studio requirement has become less relevant with the introduction of [videotape](/wiki/Videotape "Videotape") recorders in 1956 (which allowed local content to be easily generated off-site and transported to stations), the growing portability of broadcast-quality production equipment due to [transistorization](/wiki/Transistor "Transistor"), and the elimination of requirements (in 1987 for most classes of US broadcast stations) that broadcasters originate any minimum amount of local content.

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article or section **possibly contains original [synthesis](/wiki/Wikipedia:SYNTH "Wikipedia:SYNTH").** Source material should [verifiably mention](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability") and [relate](/wiki/Wikipedia:Content_removal#Irrelevant_information "Wikipedia:Content removal") to the main topic. Relevant discussion may be found on the [talk page](/wiki/Talk:City_of_license "Talk:City of license"). *(April 2010)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

While the main studio concept nominally remains in US broadcast regulations, and certain administrative requirements (such as the local employment of a manager and the equivalent of at least one other full-time staff member, as well as the maintenance of a public inspection file) are still applied, removal of the requirement that stations originate local content greatly weakens the significance of maintaining a local main studio. A facility capable of originating programming and feeding it to a [transmitter](/wiki/Transmitter "Transmitter") must still exist, but under normal conditions there most often is no requirement that these local studio actually be in active use to originate any specific local programming.

In many cases, the use of [centralcasting](/wiki/Centralcasting "Centralcasting") and [broadcast automation](/wiki/Broadcast_automation "Broadcast automation") has greatly weakened the role and importance of manual control by staff at the nominal local station studio facilities.

Exceptions to these rules have been made by regulators, primarily on a case-by-case basis, to deal with "satellite stations": transmitters which are licensed to comply with the technical requirements of [full service](/wiki/Full_service_(radio_format) "Full service (radio format)") broadcast facilities and have their own independent [call signs](/wiki/Call_sign "Call sign") and communities of license but are used simply as full-power [broadcast translators](/wiki/Broadcast_translator "Broadcast translator") to rebroadcast another station. These are most often non-commercial educational stations or stations serving thinly populated areas which otherwise would be too small to support an independent local full-service broadcaster.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

### Political considerations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_of_license&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Political considerations")]

The requirement that a full-service station maintain local presence in its community of license has been used by proponents of [localism](/wiki/Localism_(politics) "Localism (politics)") and [community broadcasting](/wiki/Community_radio "Community radio") as a means to oppose the construction and use of local stations as mere rebroadcasters or satellite-fed translators of distant stations. Without specific requirements for service to the local community of license, stations could be constructed in large number by out-of-region broadcasters who feed transmitters via [satellite](/wiki/Communications_satellite "Communications satellite") and offer no local content.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

There also has been a de facto preference by regulators to encourage the assignment of broadcast licenses to smaller cities which otherwise would have no local voice, instead of allowing all broadcast activity to be concentrated in large metropolitan areas already served by many existing broadcasters.

When dealing with multiple competing US radio station applications, current FM allotment priorities are: (1) first full-time aural service; (2) second full-time aural service; (3) first local aural transmission service; and (4) Other public interest matters.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Similar criteria were extended to competing applicants for non-commercial stations by US legislation passed in 2000.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

#### Suburban community problem

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_of_license&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Suburban community problem")]

Any policy favoring applicants for communities not already served by an existing station has had the unintended effect of encouraging applicants to merely list a small suburb of a large city, claiming to be the "first station in the community" even though the larger city is well served by many existing stations. "The Suburban Community Problem" was recognized in FCC policy as early as 1965. "Stations in metropolitan areas often tend to seek out national and regional advertisers and to identify themselves with the entire metropolitan area rather than with the particular needs of their specified communities," according to an FCC policy statement of the era. In order "to discourage applicants for smaller communities who would be merely substandard stations for neighboring, larger communities," the FCC established the so-called "Suburban Community presumption" which required applicants for AM stations in such markets to demonstrate that they had ascertained the unmet programming needs of the specific communities and were prepared to satisfy those needs.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

By 1969, the same issues had spread to FM licensing; instead of building transmitters in the community to nominally be served, applicants would often seek to locate the tower site at least halfway to the next major city. In one such precedent case (the Berwick Doctrine), the FCC required a hearing before Berwick, a prospective broadcaster, could locate transmitters midway between [Pittston, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pittston,_Pennsylvania "Pittston, Pennsylvania") (the city of license), and a larger audience in [Wilkes-Barre](/wiki/Wilkes-Barre,_Pennsylvania "Wilkes-Barre, Pennsylvania").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

A related problem was that of 'move-in'. Outlying communities would find their small-town local stations sold to outsiders, who would then attempt to change the community of license to a suburb of the nearest major city, move transmitter locations or remove existing local content from broadcasts in an attempt to move into the larger city.

The small town of [Anniston, Alabama](/wiki/Anniston,_Alabama "Anniston, Alabama"), due to its location 90 miles west of [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta") and 65 miles east of [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham,_Alabama "Birmingham, Alabama"), has lost local content from both TV and FM stations which were re-targeted at one of the two larger urban centers or moved outright. ([WHMA-FM](/wiki/WNNX "WNNX") Anniston is now licensed as WNNX [College Park, Georgia](/wiki/College_Park,_Georgia "College Park, Georgia")—an Atlanta suburb—after a failed attempt to relicense it to [Sandy Springs, Georgia](/wiki/Sandy_Springs,_Georgia "Sandy Springs, Georgia")—another [Atlanta suburb](/wiki/Metro_Atlanta#Surrounding_cities "Metro Atlanta"). Transmitters are now in downtown Atlanta.)[[16]](#cite_note-16)
The same is true for WJSU, which served East Alabama with local news until the station was merged into a triplex to form [ABC 33/40](/wiki/WBMA "WBMA") which focuses its coverage on the central part of the state.

A 1988 precedent case (Faye and Richard Tuck, 3 FCC Rcd 5374, 1988) created the "Tuck Analysis" as a standard which attempts to address the Suburban Community Problem on a case-by-case basis by examining:

1. the station's proposed signal coverage over the urbanized area (the "Coverage Factor");
2. the relative population size and distance between the suburban community and the urban market (the "Relative Size and Distance Factor"); and
3. the independence of the suburban community, based on various factors that would indicate self-sufficiency (the "Independence Factor").

Despite the best intentions of regulators, the system remains prone to manipulation.

> This has almost become a parlor game. The goal of the game—whether you're applying for a new station or a station currently licensed to a rural area—is to move as close to a big market as possible. The closer you get to a big market, the more potential listeners you can reach and hence the more advertising dollars you can attract. But there's a catch—at least there's supposed to be. The Commission is required by Section 307(b) of the Communications Act "to provide a fair, efficient, and equitable distribution of radio service" to "the several States and communities." The FCC cannot simply permit radio stations to relocate from rural areas to well-served urban markets without violating that mandate. That's when the game gets interesting. Under our FM allotment rules, the Commission will give a preference to any applicant that proposes to serve a community with no current licensees—i.e., not that the community doesn't receive radio service (it could receive service from dozens of stations) but that no station lists that particular community as its "community of license." That's where a good atlas comes in handy. The next step is to scour the maps to find a community near an urban area that doesn't yet have any stations licensed to it. You win the game if you get the FCC to grant you a preference for providing "first service" to a close-in suburban community while being able to cover the larger market.

— Commissioners Copps & Adelstein, Federal Communications Commission, 2008[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Licensing and on-air identity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_of_license&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Licensing and on-air identity")]

While becoming less meaningful over the decades, stations are still required to post a [public file](/wiki/Public_file "Public file") somewhere within 25 miles of the city, and to cover the entire city with a local [radio signal](/wiki/Radio_signal "Radio signal"). In the United States, a station's [transmitter](/wiki/Transmitter "Transmitter") must be located so that it can provide a strong signal over nearly all of its "principal community" (5 mV/m or stronger at night for AM stations, 70 dbuV for FM, 35 dbu for DTV channels 2–6, 43 dbu for channels 7-13 and 48 dbu for channels 14+), even if it primarily serves another city.[[18]](#cite_note-18) For example, American television station [WTTV](/wiki/WTTV "WTTV") primarily serves [Indianapolis](/wiki/Indianapolis "Indianapolis"); however, the transmitter is located farther south than the other stations in that city because it is licensed to [Bloomington](/wiki/Bloomington,_Indiana "Bloomington, Indiana"), 50 miles south of Indianapolis (it maintains a satellite station, WTTK, licensed to [Kokomo, Indiana](/wiki/Kokomo,_Indiana "Kokomo, Indiana"), but in the digital age, WTTK is for all intents and purposes the station's main signal, transmitting from the traditional Indianapolis transmitter site). In some cases, such as [Jeannette, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Jeannette,_Pennsylvania "Jeannette, Pennsylvania")-licensed [WPKD-TV](/wiki/WPKD-TV "WPKD-TV") 19, the FCC has waived this requirement; the station claimed that retaining an existing transmitter site 25.6 miles southeast of its new community of license of Jeannette would be in compliance with the commission's minimum distance separation requirements (avoiding interference to [co-channel](/wiki/Co-channel_interference "Co-channel interference") [WOIO](/wiki/WOIO "WOIO") 19 [Shaker Heights](/wiki/Shaker_Heights,_Ohio "Shaker Heights, Ohio")).[[19]](#cite_note-19) Another extreme example of a station's transmitter located far from the city of license is the FM station [KPNT](/wiki/KPNT "KPNT"), formerly licensed to [Ste. Genevieve, Missouri](/wiki/Ste._Genevieve,_Missouri "Ste. Genevieve, Missouri"), and transmitting from [Hillsboro](/wiki/Hillsboro,_Missouri "Hillsboro, Missouri"), but serving the [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis") and [Metro East](/wiki/Metro_East "Metro East") market to the north. In 2015, the station was allowed by the FCC to move their city of license to [Collinsville, Illinois](/wiki/Collinsville,_Illinois "Collinsville, Illinois"), and have a transmitter in St. Louis proper with a power decrease.

FCC regulations also require stations at least once an hour to state the station's call letters, followed by the city of license. However, the FCC has no restrictions on additional names after the city of license, so many stations afterwards add the nearest large city. For example, CBS affiliate [WOIO](/wiki/WOIO "WOIO") is licensed to [Shaker Heights](/wiki/Shaker_Heights,_Ohio "Shaker Heights, Ohio"), a suburb of [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland "Cleveland"), and thus identifies as "WOIO Shaker Heights-Cleveland." Similarly, northern [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)")'s [WWNY-TV](/wiki/WWNY-TV "WWNY-TV") (also a CBS affiliate) identifies as "WWNY-TV [7](/wiki/North_American_broadcast_television_frequencies "North American broadcast television frequencies") [Carthage](/wiki/Carthage,_New_York "Carthage, New York")-[Watertown](/wiki/Watertown,_New_York "Watertown, New York")" as a historical artifact; the original broadcasts originated from [Champion Hill](/wiki/Champion,_New_York "Champion, New York") in 1954 so the license still reflects this tiny location.[[a]](#cite_note-20)

If the station is licensed in the primary city served, on occasion the station will list a second city or region next to it. For example, the [Tampa Bay](/wiki/Tampa_Bay "Tampa Bay") region's Fox owned-and-operated station [WTVT](/wiki/WTVT "WTVT") is licensed to [Tampa, Florida](/wiki/Tampa,_Florida "Tampa, Florida"), its primary city, but identifies on-air as "WTVT Tampa/[St. Petersburg](/wiki/St._Petersburg,_Florida "St. Petersburg, Florida")", as St. Petersburg is another major city in the market. To encompass [Appleton](/wiki/Appleton,_Wisconsin "Appleton, Wisconsin") and the smaller cities clustered around the [Fox River](/wiki/Fox_River_(Green_Bay_tributary) "Fox River (Green Bay tributary)") southwest of [Green Bay, Wisconsin](/wiki/Green_Bay,_Wisconsin "Green Bay, Wisconsin"), stations in the Green Bay–Appleton area identify as "Green Bay/[Fox Cities](/wiki/Fox_Cities "Fox Cities")" (e.g. "[WBAY-TV](/wiki/WBAY-TV "WBAY-TV"), Green Bay/Fox Cities"); Green Bay-licensed stations thus still carry an official identification, while providing the ability for stations licensed to other places in the region to officially prefix their name before the mention of "Green Bay/Fox Cities".

There is no longer a requirement to carry [programs](/wiki/Program_(management) "Program (management)") relevant to the particular community,[[b]](#cite_note-21) or even necessarily to operate or transmit from that community. Accordingly, stations licensed to smaller communities in major [metropolitan markets](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area") often target programming toward the entire market rather than the official home community, and often move their studio facilities to the larger urban centre as well. For instance, the Canadian radio station [CFNY-FM](/wiki/CFNY-FM "CFNY-FM") is officially licensed to [Brampton, Ontario](/wiki/Brampton "Brampton"), although its studio and transmitter facilities are located in downtown [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto").

This may, at times, lead to confusion — while media directories normally list broadcast stations by their legal community of license, audiences often disregard (or may even be entirely unaware of) the distinction. For instance, for a short time while resolving a license conflict and ownership transaction in 1989, the current day [KCAL-TV](/wiki/KCAL-TV "KCAL-TV") in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") was licensed to the little-known southeast suburb of [Norwalk, California](/wiki/Norwalk,_California "Norwalk, California"), with the station's identifications at the time only vocally mentioning the temporary city of license in a rushed form, with Norwalk barely receiving any visual mention on the station; at no time were any station assets actually based in Norwalk, nor was public affairs or news programming adjusted to become Norwalk-centric over that of Los Angeles and [Southern California](/wiki/Southern_California "Southern California"). The station returned to its Los Angeles city of license after the transaction was complete.

Often, a station will keep a tiny outlying community in its licensing and on-air identity long after the original rationale for choosing that location is no longer truly applicable. [Sneedville, Tennessee](/wiki/Sneedville,_Tennessee "Sneedville, Tennessee"), as city of license for PBS member station [WETP-TV](/wiki/WETP-TV "WETP-TV") originally made sense as a compromise location to serve both [Knoxville](/wiki/Knoxville,_Tennessee "Knoxville, Tennessee") and the [Tri-Cities of Tennessee and Virginia](/wiki/Tri-Cities,_Tennessee "Tri-Cities, Tennessee") on VHF channel 2. It met the minimum distance requirements to two other channel 2 stations in the region, [WKRN](/wiki/WKRN "WKRN") in [Nashville](/wiki/Nashville "Nashville") and [WSB-TV](/wiki/WSB-TV "WSB-TV") in [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta"). This became less important after full-power UHF satellite [WKOP-TV](/wiki/WKOP-TV "WKOP-TV") signed on in Knoxville, and irrelevant once the [2003-09 DTV transition](/wiki/Digital_television_transition_in_the_United_States "Digital television transition in the United States") and [2016-21 repack](/wiki/2016_United_States_wireless_spectrum_auction#Repacking "2016 United States wireless spectrum auction") moved WETP's main signal to physical channel UHF 24. Nonetheless, broadcasters and regulatory authorities are more likely to retain the original city of license, rather than bring unwanted scrutiny for taking away a small community's only station, which may be a mark of [civic pride](/wiki/Boosterism "Boosterism"), only to move it to some larger center which already has multiple stations.

## Table of allotments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_of_license&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Table of allotments")]

In the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), the [Federal Communications Commission](/wiki/Federal_Communications_Commission "Federal Communications Commission") maintains a Table of Allotments, which assigns individual channel frequencies to individual cities or communities for both [TV](/wiki/Television "Television") and [FM radio](/wiki/FM_radio "FM radio").[[20]](#cite_note-22)

A corresponding Table of Allotments for [digital television](/wiki/Digital_television "Digital television") was created in 1997.[[21]](#cite_note-23) To operate a licensed station, a broadcaster must first obtain allocation of the desired frequencies in the FCC's Table of Allotments for the intended city of license. This process is subject to various political and bureaucratic restrictions, based on considerations including the number of existing stations in the area.[[c]](#cite_note-24)

The term "city" has in some cases been relaxed to mean "community", often including the unincorporated areas around the city that share a mailing address. This sometimes leads to inconsistencies, such as the licensing of one [metro Atlanta](/wiki/Metro_Atlanta "Metro Atlanta") station to the unincorporated [Cobb County](/wiki/Cobb_County,_Georgia "Cobb County, Georgia") community of [Mableton](/wiki/Mableton,_Georgia "Mableton, Georgia"), but the refusal to license [another](/wiki/WNNX#History "WNNX") to [Sandy Springs](/wiki/Sandy_Springs,_Georgia "Sandy Springs, Georgia"),[[22]](#cite_note-25) which is one of the largest cities in the state, and was at the time an unincorporated part of [Fulton County](/wiki/Fulton_County,_Georgia "Fulton County, Georgia") due to political complications involving its eventual incorporation from both the state and its containing county of [Fulton](/wiki/Fulton_County,_Georgia "Fulton County, Georgia").

The definition of a "community" also comes into play when a broadcaster wants to take a station away from a tiny hamlet like [North Pole, New York](/wiki/North_Pole,_New_York "North Pole, New York"), whose population is in decline. In general, regulators are loath to allow a community's only license to be moved away - especially to a city which already has a station.[[d]](#cite_note-26) A broadcaster may make the case that the "community" functionally no longer exists in order to be released from its local obligations.

Often, the city of license does not correspond to the location of the station itself, of the primary audience or of the communities identified in the station's branding and advertising.

Some of the more common reasons for a community of license to be listed as a point far from the actual audience[[e]](#cite_note-27) include:

### The "compromise" location

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_of_license&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: The \"compromise\" location")]

A broadcaster may wish to serve two different communities, both in the same region but far enough from each other that a transmitter in one market would provide poor service to the other. While a transmitter in each community served would be preferable, occasionally a station licensed to a small town between the two larger centres will be used.

Both cities are now served by local FM repeaters of CBK, yet the original clear-channel AM 540 and the community of license remain with tiny Watrous. |

### The suburban station

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_of_license&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: The suburban station")]

In [FM radio](/wiki/FM_radio "FM radio") broadcasting, small local stations were sometimes built to serve [suburban](/wiki/Suburb "Suburb") or outlying areas in an era where [AM radio](/wiki/AM_radio "AM radio") stations held the largest audiences and much of the FM spectrum lay vacant. In the era of [vacuum tubes](/wiki/Vacuum_tube "Vacuum tube"), the five-tube AM radio with no FM tuning capability and limited audio quality was common; later advances in receiver design were to make good-quality FM commonplace (even though most AM/FM stereo receivers still have severely limited AM frequency response and no [AM stereo](/wiki/AM_stereo "AM stereo") decoders). Eventually FM spectrum became a very scarce commodity in many markets as AM stations moved to the FM dial, relegating AM largely to [talk radio](/wiki/Talk_radio "Talk radio"). As cities expanded, former small-town FM stations found themselves not only in what were now becoming rapidly expanding suburbs but also on what was becoming some of the most valuable spectrum in broadcast radio. The once-tiny FM stations would often then be sold, increased (where possible) to much-higher power and used to serve a huge mainstream audience in the larger metropolitan area.

The station has since changed hands various times in the 1980s and now broadcasts from Toronto's [CN Tower](/wiki/CN_Tower "CN Tower") with a [modern rock](/wiki/Modern_rock "Modern rock") format. ### The short-spaced station

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_of_license&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: The short-spaced station")]

To avoid [co-channel interference](/wiki/Co-channel_interference "Co-channel interference"), a minimum distance is maintained between stations operating on the same frequency in different markets. On [VHF](/wiki/VHF "VHF"), full-power stations are typically 175 miles or more apart before the same channel is used again. An otherwise-desirable channel may therefore be unavailable to a community unless either it is operated at greatly reduced-height and power, forced onto a strongly directional antenna pattern to protect the distant co-channel station or relocated to some other, more distant location in the region to maintain proper spacing. The choice of another community as home for a station can be one possible means to avoid short-spacing, effectively shifting the entire station's coverage area to maintain the required distances between transmitters.

### The distant mountaintop antenna

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_of_license&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: The distant mountaintop antenna")]

In hilly or mountainous regions, a city would often be built in a waterfront or lakeside location (such as [Plattsburgh](/wiki/Plattsburgh,_New_York "Plattsburgh, New York")-[Burlington](/wiki/Burlington,_Vermont "Burlington, Vermont"), both on [Lake Champlain](/wiki/Lake_Champlain "Lake Champlain")) - lower ground which in turn would be surrounded by tall mountain peaks. The only reliable means to get the [VHF](/wiki/VHF "VHF") television or radio signals over the mountains was to build a station atop one of the mountain peaks. This occasionally left stations with a distant mountaintop (or its nearest small crossroads) as the historical city of license, even though the audience was elsewhere.

| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [WPTZ](/wiki/WPTZ "WPTZ") 5 [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC") | [Plattsburgh](/wiki/Plattsburgh,_New_York "Plattsburgh, New York") | [North Pole, New York](/wiki/North_Pole,_New_York "North Pole, New York") | WPTZ was originally licensed in 1954 to [North Pole, New York](/wiki/North_Pole,_New_York "North Pole, New York"), the closest tiny crossroads to its [mountaintop](/wiki/Mountain "Mountain") transmitter site near [Lake Placid](/wiki/Lake_Placid,_New_York "Lake Placid, New York")/[Adirondack State Park](/wiki/Adirondack_State_Park "Adirondack State Park"). The station then used "North Pole–Plattsburgh–[Burlington](/wiki/Burlington,_Vermont "Burlington, Vermont")" or even "[Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal")" as part of its on-air identity but the community of license, once chosen, is not easily modified. The station moved its transmitter to [Mount Mansfield](/wiki/Mount_Mansfield "Mount Mansfield"), Vermont, in the digital age to centralize its signal with the rest of the market's stations licensed east of [Lake Champlain](/wiki/Lake_Champlain "Lake Champlain"), and in January 2011, the city of license was authorized to become Plattsburgh.[[30]](#cite_note-38)[[31]](#cite_note-39) In 2019, the station relocated their main studios to [South Burlington, Vermont](/wiki/South_Burlington,_Vermont "South Burlington, Vermont"), keeping a relocated and downsized news bureau and backup facility in Plattsburgh.[[32]](#cite_note-40)[[33]](#cite_note-41) |

### The relocation of an existing station

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_of_license&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: The relocation of an existing station")]

Often, a license for a new station will not be available in a community, either because a regulatory agency was only willing to accept new applications within specified narrow timeframes or because there are no suitable vacant channels. A prospective broadcaster must therefore buy an existing station as the only way to readily enter the market, in some cases being left with a station in a suburban, outlying or adjacent-market area if that were the only facility available for sale.

It was eventually acquired by [CHUM Limited](/wiki/CHUM_Limited "CHUM Limited") in 1997, and added an over-the-air transmitter in Ottawa after joining CHUM's [NewNet](/wiki/NewNet "NewNet") system. CHUM subsequently centralized the station's operations and studios in Ottawa. The Ottawa transmitter is [1080i 16:9 digital widescreen](/wiki/CHRO-TV#Digital_television_and_high_definition "CHRO-TV") and, on paper at least, purports to be rebroadcasting CHRO (VHF 5, a standard-definition analog station) from Pembroke. ### The border blaster

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_of_license&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: The border blaster")]

Occasionally, a community on an international border is served using a [station licensed to another country](/wiki/Border_blaster "Border blaster"). This may provide access to less restrictive broadcast regulation or represent a means to use [local marketing agreements](/wiki/Local_marketing_agreement "Local marketing agreement") or adjacent-market licenses to circumvent limits on the number of stations under common ownership.

### The last-available frequency allocation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_of_license&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: The last-available frequency allocation")]

In the early days of television, the majority of stations could be found on the [VHF](/wiki/VHF "VHF") band; in North America, this currently represents just twelve possible channels and in large markets any suitable allocations in this range were mostly full by the early 1950s. Occasionally, a prospective broadcaster could obtain one of these coveted positions by acquiring an existing station or permit in an adjacent community - although in some cases this meant a move out-of-state.

### The new entrant

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_of_license&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: The new entrant")]

A new network or station group will often enter a market after all of the most valuable available frequencies (such as the analogue VHF TV assignments in major cities) are already taken. This often results in building a network by constructing outlying stations, UHF stations, underpowered stations or some mix of all three. That can leave transmitters licensed to some very strange or tiny places. This happened to some degree with networks which signed on in the 1960s, such as [National Educational Television](/wiki/National_Educational_Television "National Educational Television") in the US or the [CTV Television Network](/wiki/CTV_Television_Network "CTV Television Network") in Canada. Later entrants fared worse.

In the U.S., [PAX Network](/wiki/PAX_Network "PAX Network") (now [Ion Television](/wiki/Ion_Television "Ion Television")) was prone to this, building a network largely from outlying [owned-and-operated](/wiki/Owned-and-operated_station "Owned-and-operated station") UHF stations.

In Canada, third networks such as [Global](/wiki/Global_Television_Network "Global Television Network") were often a motley collection of outlying stations in their early years. [CKGN-TV](/wiki/CIII-TV "CIII-TV"), Ontario's original "Global Television Network" repeater chain, signed on in 1974 in an already densely packed stretch of the beaten-path Windsor-Quebec corridor in which few desirable channels were available. Cities such as [Windsor](/wiki/Windsor,_Ontario "Windsor, Ontario"), [London](/wiki/London,_Ontario "London, Ontario"), [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto,_Ontario "Toronto, Ontario"), [Peterborough](/wiki/Peterborough,_Ontario "Peterborough, Ontario"), [Kingston](/wiki/Kingston,_Ontario "Kingston, Ontario") and [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall,_Ontario "Cornwall, Ontario")[[k]](#cite_note-58) are notable by their absence from the network's original roster.[[48]](#cite_note-59) The five transmitters on-air in 1984 (after a decade of operation as a struggling "third network") were:

:   * [Sarnia](/wiki/Sarnia,_Ontario "Sarnia, Ontario") transmitting from Oil Springs on UHF 29 (370kW)
    * [Paris](/wiki/Paris,_Ontario "Paris, Ontario") on VHF 6 (at the full 100kW, the most allocated to a station of this class in Ontario)
    * [Uxbridge](/wiki/Uxbridge,_Ontario "Uxbridge, Ontario") on UHF 22 (at the full 5000kW, the most powerful in the nation, but on an undesirable suburban UHF allocation nowhere near downtown Toronto)
    * [Bancroft](/wiki/Bancroft,_Ontario "Bancroft, Ontario") on VHF 2 (at 87kW - and later increased to the full 100kW, but in the speck-on-a-map unincorporated hamlet of [Vennachar](/wiki/Addington_Highlands "Addington Highlands"), near Denbigh).
    * [Ottawa](/wiki/Ottawa,_Ontario "Ottawa, Ontario") on VHF 6 (at one-eighth the typical power for a station in its class, and on a sharp directional pattern focussed on Ottawa). This station had to protect [CBMT](/wiki/CBMT "CBMT") (VHF 6, [CBC Montréal](/wiki/Canadian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Canadian Broadcasting Corporation")) less than 120 miles distant - and this at a time when full-power VHF TV co-channel stations were typically spaced 175-200 miles (280-320km) apart to prevent interference.

The majority of these transmitters were not licensed to the primary community served. Many were underpowered, short-spaced or in undesirable locations - often just putting enough signal into key communities to obtain cable [must-carry](/wiki/Must-carry "Must-carry") protection. As the only transmitters to be operating on then-valuable VHF channels at anything other than greatly reduced power were licensed to Paris and Bancroft, both awkward outlying communities, the Paris transmitter was arbitrarily listed as the main station for the entire network.

### The cable or digital TV placeholder

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_of_license&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: The cable or digital TV placeholder")]

Sometimes, putting a usable over-the-air signal into the primary community served is anywhere from second-priority to not a priority at all. A station could be [barely within](/wiki/Rimshot_(broadcasting) "Rimshot (broadcasting)") the [market's boundaries](/wiki/Designated_Market_Area "Designated Market Area") or be underpowered to the point of putting a "B" grade signal into the community at best. On anything less than a huge rooftop antenna, the station is unwatchable — but, even if the underlying over-the-air signal was not valuable, the corresponding [cable television](/wiki/Cable_television "Cable television") slots in the various communities it was almost serving were. Any full-service domestic signal above some arbitrary minimum had access to "[must carry](/wiki/Must_carry "Must carry")" protection, could request favourable placement on the dial and (in Canada) could engage in [signal substitution](/wiki/Signal_substitution "Signal substitution") to take ad revenue from other stations already carrying the same content.

The [2016-2020 OTA TV repack](/wiki/2016_United_States_wireless_spectrum_auction "2016 United States wireless spectrum auction") opened additional possibilities for using an outlying community's licence as an over-the-air placeholder. Buy a station, return the licensed broadcast spectrum to the government, then claim to be "sharing" a channel with another broadcaster by using the orphan licence to place content on one of their [digital subchannels](/wiki/Digital_subchannel "Digital subchannel"). Suddenly, an outlying commercial low-power station in [New Hampshire](/wiki/New_Hampshire "New Hampshire") is "sharing" space on [WGBX](/wiki/WGBX "WGBX"), a full-power non-commercial station in the heart of the [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston") market. The same transmitter can, by using two different licences in a "channel sharing" arrangement, have two different communities of licence - which may allow more flexibility for its location. It is also possible to mix commercial and non-commercial licences. In Canada, where [CRTC](/wiki/CRTC "CRTC") regulations prevent carrying any additional, unique programming on digital subchannels without obtaining a second licence (and taking all the obligations which go with it) for each subchannel, returning just the spectrum (and keeping the licence) can be used as a means to recycle licences from abandoned, defunct outlying stations for use elsewhere in the network.

### The use of an adjacent market

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_of_license&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: The use of an adjacent market")]

Occasionally, a station owner would reach a legal limit on [concentration of media ownership](/wiki/Concentration_of_media_ownership "Concentration of media ownership"), already having the maximum number of commonly owned stations in a market. Additional stations would be possible by transmitting the extra signals from a station technically in an adjacent market.

The city of license has therefore remained at Smiths Falls, a small town of roughly 80 km distant, and the station was absent from the (now-defunct) Ottawa-Hull [digital radio](/wiki/Digital_Audio_Broadcasting "Digital Audio Broadcasting") cluster as that signal would not reach the community of license.[[50]](#cite_note-63) Despite this the station effectively acts as an adjunct to the Ottawa radio market, and has undergone frequent format, branding and call sign changes based on market conditions in Ottawa.[[n]](#cite_note-64) Like its nominally Smiths Falls-licensed sibling [CJET-FM](/wiki/CJET-FM "CJET-FM"), CKBY's transmitters are actually in [Carleton Place, Ontario](/wiki/Carleton_Place,_Ontario "Carleton Place, Ontario") - roughly halfway from Smiths Falls to Ottawa. |

A [Greater Toronto Area](/wiki/Greater_Toronto_Area "Greater Toronto Area") community could have reasonably been chosen as nominal city of license, but to do so would be to name a suburban [UHF](/wiki/UHF "UHF") outlet (22 [Uxbridge](/wiki/Uxbridge,_Ontario "Uxbridge, Ontario")) as the main station. Eventually UHF's perceived disadvantage was diminished by cable and the start of the digital era. Meanwhile, CIII-TV obtained a Toronto allocation (UHF 41) and took the outlying station dark (the UHF 22 allocation later went to [CHEX-2 Durham](/wiki/CHEX-DT-2 "CHEX-DT-2"), but at much less power[[p]](#cite_note-66)). The station's Toronto rebroadcaster on channel 41 (CIII-TV-41) became the originating broadcaster legally as defined by the CRTC for the CIII/Global Ontario network in mid-2009.[[51]](#cite_note-relicense-67) |
| [CKMI-TV](/wiki/CKMI-TV "CKMI-TV") 20 [Global](/wiki/Global_Television_Network "Global Television Network") | [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal") | [Quebec City](/wiki/Quebec_City "Quebec City") | Similarly, from the station's launch until 2009, Quebec was the city of license and Montreal 46 / [Sherbrooke](/wiki/Sherbrooke "Sherbrooke") 11 merely repeaters constructed after acquisition of the existing Quebec station. As the studios, master control facilities and largest audience are in Montreal, and the number of anglophones in mostly francophone Quebec City is small, this was a Montreal station in all but name. Like its sister station CIII, the station's license was moved to Montreal in 2009.[[51]](#cite_note-relicense-67) |

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_of_license&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-20)** It is possible for two stations to have the same studio location and transmit from the same mast at the same site, but be licensed to different communities; [WWNY-TV](/wiki/WWNY-TV "WWNY-TV") and [WNYF-CD](/wiki/WNYF-CD "WNYF-CD") (Carthage and Watertown NY, respectively) are one example.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-21)** There are a few rare exceptions, even in the US. US low-power FM stations were originally introduced with far more stringent broadcast localism requirements than any other station class. Broadcast regulators may also add extra restrictions to one specific licence: [13 Newark](/wiki/WNET "WNET") was only allowed to move its facilities to [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") on condition that the licence, station ID and 2.5 hours/week of community programming remain with [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"). That local programming remains today. Non-US stations are subject to their own nation's rules — a full-power rebroadcaster is easier to licence in Canada or México, but an originating station in Canada must gather local news.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-24)** It is possible for an applicant to petition the FCC for rulemaking to change the table of allotments, but it is a long bureaucratic process. The [original 1952 allotments](https://web.archive.org/web/20080517113019/http://www.w9wi.com/articles/1952.htm) for [New York State TV](https://web.archive.org/web/20110515212431/http://www.w9wi.com/images/1952allotments4.jpg) had 2,4,7,17,\*23,59 (\* non-commercial education) allocated to Buffalo or Buffalo-Niagara, while Watertown is only allocated 48. An applicant who wanted to build 7 in Watertown, for example, would encounter multiple obstacles: They'd have to petition the FCC to change the table of allotments. They'd have to show adequate distance from existing same-channel allocations (in those days, 175 miles, straight-line). They'd have to show adequate distance from adjacent-channel stations (so 7 or 9 couldn't have been assigned to Syracuse without some major reshuffling of existing channels if 8 was CBS in that city way back [WHEN](/wiki/WTVH "WTVH")). Within 200 miles of the Canada or Mexico border? Everything becomes subject to international co-ordination. Find your tiny spot 175 miles from Buffalo and nowhere near anything else on 7 or 8, jump through all the bureaucratic hoops to add that community to the Table of Allotments, get that channel allotted to that community, convince the FCC that your application is in the public interest and eventually, maybe your [channel 7](/wiki/WWNY-TV "WWNY-TV") will flicker to life, live long and prosper. A more complex application (such as the attempts to shoehorn a third VHF into Syracuse and Rochester for ABC in the 1960s) might involve swapping allocations between multiple communities or even displacing existing stations in frequency or location. For instance, if Syracuse already had [CBS 8](/wiki/WTVH "WTVH"), poaching a vacant 9 Elmira and moving that allocation to Syracuse required that [8 Syracuse](/wiki/WTVH "WTVH") swap channels with [5 Rochester](/wiki/WROC-TV "WROC-TV"), which then allowed [a Toronto station](/wiki/CBLT "CBLT") to move to 5, which then left 6 vacant for a Canadian [broadcaster](/wiki/CIII-DT#Transmitters "CIII-DT") or [two](/wiki/CJOH-DT#Transmitters "CJOH-DT").
4. **[^](#cite_ref-26)** A rare few exceptions were made to accommodate the then-fledgling third-rank [American Broadcasting Company](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company") in the 1950s and early 1960s. There was also a loophole to accommodate stations which no longer reached their original communities of license after having sold their spectrum in the [2016 United States wireless spectrum auction](/wiki/2016_United_States_wireless_spectrum_auction "2016 United States wireless spectrum auction")).
5. **[^](#cite_ref-27)** There is also one special case involving the [Windsor](/wiki/Windsor,_Ontario "Windsor, Ontario")-[Detroit](/wiki/Detroit,_Michigan "Detroit, Michigan") market. Licensing a station to Windsor carries a unique obstacle - content which is already on the Detroit stations will not be provided to a Canadian station in the same market, even though Windsor is in a different country. Using an outlying station just outside the Windsor-Essex market boundaries may therefore allow access to programming which might not be available to a Windsor-licensed station.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-29)** Watertown NY in 1952-54 would have been free to request allotment of any full-power VHF TV channel which is not co-channel to [2 Montréal](/wiki/CBFT-DT "CBFT-DT"), [2 Buffalo](/wiki/WGRZ-TV "WGRZ-TV"), [3 Syracuse](/wiki/WSTM-TV "WSTM-TV"), [3 Burlington](/wiki/WCAX "WCAX"), [4 Ottawa](/wiki/CBOT-DT "CBOT-DT"), [4 Buffalo](/wiki/WIVB-TV "WIVB-TV"), [4 Albany](/wiki/WRGB "WRGB"), [5 Rochester](/wiki/WROC-TV "WROC-TV"), [5 North Pole](/wiki/WPTZ "WPTZ"), [6 Montreal](/wiki/CBMT-DT "CBMT-DT"), [7 Buffalo](/wiki/WKBW-TV "WKBW-TV"), [8 Syracuse](/wiki/WTVH "WTVH"), [8 Cornwall](/wiki/CJSS-TV "CJSS-TV"), [9 Ottawa](/wiki/CBOFT-DT "CBOFT-DT"), [10 Rochester](/wiki/WHEC-TV "WHEC-TV"), [10 Montréal](/wiki/CFTM-DT "CFTM-DT"), [10 Albany](/wiki/WTEN "WTEN"), [11 Kingston](/wiki/CKWS-DT "CKWS-DT"), [12 Binghamton](/wiki/WBNG-TV "WBNG-TV"), [12 Montreal](/wiki/CFCF-DT "CFCF-DT") or [13 Utica](/wiki/WKTV "WKTV"). There were also minimum distances between adjacent-channel stations.
7. **[^](#cite_ref-31)** Many early permits were left unbuilt and eventually cancelled as the vast majority of the UHF pioneers who launched during the 1952 land rush were already [out of business](/wiki/Dark_(broadcasting) "Dark (broadcasting)"), most within the first year. The [All-Channel Receiver Act](/wiki/All-Channel_Receiver_Act "All-Channel Receiver Act") required UHF tuners in all 1964-model or later US TV receivers; that's too late for many stations which launched in the 1950s. ACRA did not apply to Canada, where 12-channel TV's continued to be sold through the 1960s.
8. **[^](#cite_ref-33)** Watertown's market boundaries are drawn to include [Massena](/wiki/Massena,_New_York "Massena, New York"), some 90mi distant. A single full-power UHF transmitter is not enough to cover the entire market. Non-commercial [WPBS-TV](/wiki/WPBS-TV "WPBS-TV") avoided this issue by signing on two full-power transmitters (Watertown and Norwood) with identical content. A commercial station likely could not do the same without proof of economic hardship in some form. [KVRR](/wiki/KVRR "KVRR") (four full-power VHF transmitters) is one such commercial station, but only qualifies due to an extreme rural location on the North Dakota border.
9. **[^](#cite_ref-43)** Much of the early content was sponsor-controlled, created by ad agencies or produced on a low budget. ABC TV (US) likely only survived the 1950s due to an injection of equity from United Paramount Theatres and because of the demise of the fourth-ranked [DuMont Television Network](/wiki/DuMont_Television_Network "DuMont Television Network"). See <http://uhfhistory.com/documents/Silverman_Thesis_ABC.pdf> for an overview.
10. **[^](#cite_ref-44)** At the time, [TV 8 Muskogee](/wiki/KTUL "KTUL") couldn't share the same base callsign as [KTUL radio](/wiki/KTBZ_(AM) "KTBZ (AM)") unless they had the same owner and same community of licence. This restriction, which no longer exists in the US, affected many other established radio broadcasters who'd looked elsewhere for an outlying VHF TV slot, including [WHYY](/wiki/WHYY-TV "WHYY-TV")/[WUHY](/wiki/WHYY-FM "WHYY-FM") Wilmington/Philadelphia and [WCNY](/wiki/WWNY-TV "WWNY-TV")/[WWNY](/wiki/WTNY "WTNY") Carthage/Watertown.
11. **[^](#cite_ref-58)** Canada's broadcast regulator allows existing broadcasters in a market to oppose applications from new entrants if the competition would harm the existing station. Any attempt to locate in Kingston would likely be opposed by that city's lone originating station. CIII-TV never got a Cornwall transmitter as that city is in the Montréal market, which was subject to a moratorium on new entrants at the time.
12. **[^](#cite_ref-60)** Using the B-grade contour of a full-power low-VHF analogue station to rimshot multiple communities creates a problem due to the digital television transition. The digital signal typically cannot replicate the original analogue low-VHF coverage area. The communities which the station "almost" reached in analogue will not be reached at all. Hence the temptation to not convert a station like CIII-TV-2, to defer its conversion through administrative means, relocate it or take it dark.
13. **[^](#cite_ref-62)** The dealings between [Quebecor](/wiki/Quebecor "Quebecor") and rival CKWS TV are complex. Parent station [CKWS-DT](/wiki/CKWS-DT "CKWS-DT") 11 was supposed to move to DT13 to protect [Télé-Métropole](/wiki/CFTM-DT "CFTM-DT"), [TVA's](/wiki/TVA_(Canadian_TV_network) "TVA (Canadian TV network)") flagship French-language Montréal station, once that station is repacked from DT10 to DT11. In an unusual move (given the long history of Kingston-market VHF being pushed west/northwest to outlying communities to protect Montreal stations) the CRTC allowed CKWS to stay on DT11 and dump a limited amount of [co-channel interference](/wiki/Co-channel_interference "Co-channel interference") on the TVA owned-and-operated flagship. Québécor owns both Vidéotron cable and the TVA network, which may explain their desire not to carry CKWS-DT-2 on Vidéotron in Gatineau. The station that CKWS-TV claims to be trying to protect by staying on DT11? [WNYI](/wiki/WNYI "WNYI"), a satellite-fed repeater of [Daystar](/wiki/Daystar_(TV_network) "Daystar (TV network)") which rebroadcasts brokered religious programming and originates nothing, but which nominally holds a full-service VHF TV license. As one further wrinkle, leaving CKWS-DT on 11 means that commonly owned-and-operated CIII-TV-2 Bancroft cannot go digital as its assigned repack channel was DT11. Instead, it will go dark; its licence will be assigned to a digital subchannel on CKWS's [Wolfe Island, Ontario](/wiki/Wolfe_Island,_Ontario "Wolfe Island, Ontario"), transmitter... which does not reach Bancroft over-the-air.
14. **[^](#cite_ref-64)** As [Smiths Falls, Ontario](/wiki/Smiths_Falls,_Ontario "Smiths Falls, Ontario"), is legally not in the [Ottawa](/wiki/Ottawa "Ottawa") radio market, attempts by the Smiths Falls station to oppose proposed format changes by its Ottawa rivals have been rejected by the CRTC; likewise, any attempts by the Ottawa stations to oppose format changes in Smiths Falls have by this precedent also been dismissed - a situation which favours the Smiths Falls broadcaster by giving it added autonomy it would not have had in the city.
15. **[^](#cite_ref-65)** Occasionally, individual stations in a full-power rebroadcaster chain will be licensed to outlying communities to ensure spacing which locates them to avoid unnecessary overlap with other stations in the same group.
16. **[^](#cite_ref-66)** [CHEX-TV-2](/wiki/CHEX-TV-2 "CHEX-TV-2") had 5500 watts of analogue UHF, the digital signal is 185 watts - a tiny amount for what is nominally the only originating OTA TV station licensed to a beaten-path industrial city of more than 160,000 people. This tiny signal does, however, get CHEX a slot on digital cable in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto"), the largest city in the nation.
